usage_notes: The most common informal greeting in German, used at any time of day.
common_collocations: Hallo sagen, Hallo zusammen
image_search_query: two people waving hello street smile
image_context: Two people greeting each other with a wave and smile
example_2: Hallo zusammen, willkommen hier!
example_3: Er sagt immer Hallo, wenn er ankommt.
example_3_translation: Ele sempre diz olá quando chega.
pronunciation_tips: Stress on the second syllable: ha-LO
memory_hook: Sounds almost identical to English 'hello'
common_mistakes: Confusing with the formal 'Guten Tag' — 'Hallo' is casual

usage_notes: Casual farewell used among friends, family, and peers.
common_collocations: Tschüss sagen, Tschüss machen
image_search_query: person waving goodbye door leaving smile
image_context: A person waving goodbye at a door
example_2: Sie sagt Tschüss und geht nach Hause.
example_2_translation: Ela diz tchau e vai para casa.
example_3: Tschüss, es war schön mit dir!
example_3_translation: Tchau, foi ótimo estar com você!
pronunciation_tips: The 'tsch' combination sounds like English 'ch' in 'cheese'
memory_hook: Related to Italian 'ciao' — both are informal farewells
common_mistakes: Using 'Tschüss' in formal contexts where 'Auf Wiedersehen' is expected

usage_notes: Formal farewell; literally 'until we see each other again'. Used in professional and formal settings.
common_collocations: Auf Wiedersehen sagen, freundliches Auf Wiedersehen
image_search_query: business meeting handshake farewell office formal
image_context: Two people shaking hands and saying goodbye in a formal setting
example_2: Sie sagt höflich Auf Wiedersehen.
example_3_translation: Até logo e boa viagem!
pronunciation_tips: Four syllables: auf-WIE-der-se-hen; 'ie' sounds like English 'ee'
memory_hook: 'Wieder' = again, 'sehen' = to see — 'until we see again'
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'ie' as two separate vowels instead of /iː/

usage_notes: Used from waking until approximately noon. Works in both formal and informal contexts.
common_collocations: Guten Morgen wünschen, guten Morgen sagen
image_search_query: sunrise morning coffee cup warm light kitchen
image_context: A person enjoying a cup of coffee by a sunny morning window
example_1_translation: Bom dia, você ainda está dormindo?
example_2: Guten Morgen, wie war die Nacht?
example_2_translation: Bom dia, como foi a noite?
example_3_translation: Ele diz 'bom dia' toda manhã.
pronunciation_tips: 'G' in 'Guten' is hard /ɡ/, not like English 'j'
memory_hook: 'Morgen' looks like 'morning'; 'gut' = good → 'good morning'
related_words: der Morgen, Guten Tag, Gute Nacht
common_mistakes: Using 'Guten Morgen' after noon — use 'Guten Tag' instead

usage_notes: Standard neutral greeting usable from noon onwards; also appropriate in formal contexts at any daytime hour.
common_collocations: Guten Tag sagen, herzliches Guten Tag
image_search_query: daytime sunny city street people walking greeting
image_context: People greeting each other on a sunny street during the day
example_1_translation: Bom dia/Boa tarde, eu sou Anna.
example_2: Guten Tag, was kann ich für Sie tun?
example_2_translation: Bom dia, o que posso fazer por você?
example_3_translation: Ele cumprimenta com 'bom dia'.
pronunciation_tips: Final 'g' in 'Tag' is pronounced /k/ (devoiced)
memory_hook: 'Tag' = day in German; 'gut' = good → 'good day'
related_words: der Tag, Guten Morgen, Guten Abend
common_mistakes: Final 'g' is often mispronounced as /ɡ/ instead of /k/

usage_notes: Used from approximately 6 PM onward. Both formal and informal.
common_collocations: Guten Abend wünschen, einen schönen Abend
image_search_query: sunset city lights evening dinner table candle
image_context: A candlelit dinner table at dusk with city lights in the background
example_1: Guten Abend, wie war dein Tag?
example_1_translation: Boa noite, como foi o seu dia?
example_2: Guten Abend, darf ich mich setzen?
example_3: Sie wünscht ihm einen guten Abend.
example_3_translation: Ela deseja a ele uma boa noite.
pronunciation_tips: 'Abend' ends with /t/; the 'nd' cluster devoices the final consonant
memory_hook: 'Abend' is related to English 'even' (as in evening)
related_words: der Abend, Gute Nacht, Guten Tag
common_mistakes: Confusing 'Guten Abend' (evening greeting) with 'Gute Nacht' (bedtime farewell)

usage_notes: Said when parting at night or when going to sleep. Not a greeting — only a farewell.
common_collocations: Gute Nacht wünschen, Gute Nacht sagen
image_search_query: person sleeping bed moon night dark bedroom
image_context: A person going to sleep with moonlight through the bedroom window
example_2: Sie sagt Gute Nacht und schläft.
example_2_translation: Ela diz boa noite e dorme.
example_3_translation: Boa noite, até amanhã cedo!
pronunciation_tips: 'Nacht' contains the guttural /x/ as in Scottish 'loch'
memory_hook: 'Nacht' relates to 'night'; 'gut' = good → 'good night'
related_words: die Nacht, schlafen, Guten Abend, der Mond
common_mistakes: Using 'Gute Nacht' as a greeting rather than a farewell

usage_notes: Multi-purpose politeness word: used to make requests, to respond to 'Danke' (you're welcome), or when handing something over.
common_collocations: Bitte sehr, bitte schön, wie bitte?
image_search_query: hand offering help please polite gesture
image_context: A person offering something with an open hand and a polite smile
example_1_translation: Você pode me ajudar, por favor?
example_2: Bitte sehr! — Danke schön!
example_3_translation: Um copo de água, por favor.
pronunciation_tips: Two syllables: BIT-te; the final 'e' is a schwa /ə/
memory_hook: Think of 'bid' — you bid (ask) politely with 'bitte'
common_mistakes: Forgetting that 'bitte' also means 'you're welcome', not just 'please'

usage_notes: The standard expression of thanks. 'Danke schön' or 'Danke sehr' add more warmth.
common_collocations: Danke schön, Danke sehr, vielen Dank
image_search_query: smile thank you gratitude hands together warm
image_context: A person smiling and pressing hands together in a thank-you gesture
example_2: Vielen Dank, das ist sehr nett.
example_3_translation: Ela diz obrigada e sorri.
pronunciation_tips: 'ng' in 'Danke' is a nasal velar /ŋ/, as in English 'sing'
memory_hook: English 'thank' and German 'Dank' share a Germanic root
common_mistakes: Saying 'Danke' without a reply; response is typically 'Bitte' or 'Bitte sehr'

usage_notes: Used both to get attention and to apologise. Also functions as a noun (die Entschuldigung = the apology).
common_collocations: um Entschuldigung bitten, eine Entschuldigung annehmen
image_search_query: person apologising hand raise crowd polite sorry
image_context: A person raising a hand and apologising politely in a crowd
example_1_translation: Com licença, onde fica a estação de trem?
example_2: Entschuldigung, das war mein Fehler.
example_3_translation: Ele pede desculpas.
pronunciation_tips: Five syllables: ent-SCHUL-di-gung; stress on the second syllable
memory_hook: Contains 'Schuld' (guilt/fault) — you're saying 'without fault / excuse my fault'
common_mistakes: Over-shortening to 'Schuldigung' — acceptable colloquially but avoid in formal contexts

usage_notes: Basic affirmative response. Can also act as a discourse particle meaning 'you know' or 'indeed'.
common_collocations: ja genau, ja bitte, ja natürlich
image_search_query: person nodding yes agreement thumbs up smile
image_context: A person nodding and giving a thumbs-up in agreement
example_2: Kommst du? — Ja, natürlich!
example_3_translation: Sim, isso está certo.
pronunciation_tips: 'J' in German is always pronounced /j/ as in English 'yes', never /dʒ/
memory_hook: Short and universal; 'ja' sounds like the English letter 'ya'
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'J' as in English 'jam' — it should be /j/ as in 'year'

usage_notes: Standard negative response. Used for complete negation of a sentence or offer.
common_collocations: Nein danke, nein leider, auf keinen Fall
image_search_query: person shaking head no refusal hand wave
image_context: A person shaking their head and waving their hand to decline
example_2: Nein danke, ich habe schon gegessen.
example_3: Ist das richtig? — Nein, leider nicht.
example_3_translation: Isso está certo? — Não, infelizmente não.
pronunciation_tips: Rhymes with English 'mine'; the diphthong /aɪ̯/ slides from 'ah' to 'ee'
memory_hook: Rhymes with English 'nine' — say 'nine' without the 't'
common_mistakes: Confusing 'Nein' (no) with 'nicht' (not) — 'Nein' negates the whole statement

usage_notes: Formal version. Use 'Wie geht es dir?' for informal/familiar contexts.
common_collocations: Wie geht es Ihnen heute?, Danke, gut
image_search_query: formal handshake office meeting professional greeting
image_context: Two professionals shaking hands at a formal meeting
example_1: Guten Tag, wie geht es Ihnen?
example_1_translation: Bom dia, como vai o senhor/a senhora?
example_2: Wie geht es Ihnen heute?
example_3: Danke, mir geht es gut. Und Ihnen?
example_3_translation: Obrigado, estou bem. E o senhor?
pronunciation_tips: 'Ihnen' capitalised because it is a formal pronoun (Sie); stress on 'IH-nen'
memory_hook: 'Wie' = how, 'geht' = goes, 'Ihnen' = to you — 'how goes it to you?'
related_words: gut, schlecht, es geht, mir geht es
common_mistakes: Using lowercase 'ihnen' (to them) when meaning formal 'to you'

usage_notes: Informal version used with friends, family, and peers. Use 'Wie geht es Ihnen?' in formal contexts.
common_collocations: Wie geht's? (colloquial shortening), Danke, gut
image_search_query: friends chatting coffee casual smile conversation
image_context: Two friends chatting casually over coffee
example_2: Wie geht es dir heute?
example_3: Gut, danke! Und wie geht es dir?
example_3_translation: Bem, obrigado! E você, como vai?
pronunciation_tips: 'dir' rhymes with English 'dear'; the 'r' is uvular /ʁ/
memory_hook: Same structure as formal version but with 'dir' (informal you) instead of 'Ihnen'
common_mistakes: Using 'dir' in formal situations — always switch to 'Ihnen' with strangers and superiors

usage_notes: Standard positive response to 'Wie geht es dir/Ihnen?'. Literally 'to me it goes well'.
common_collocations: Danke, mir geht es gut; mir geht es sehr gut
image_search_query: person smiling thumbs up content happy outdoors
image_context: A person smiling contentedly outdoors with a thumbs-up
example_2: Mir geht es heute sehr gut.
example_3: Und dir? — Mir geht es gut, danke.
example_3_translation: E você? — Estou bem, obrigado.
pronunciation_tips: Dative 'mir' = /mɪʁ/; the word order is dative-before-nominative (mir before es)
memory_hook: 'gut' = good (cognate); 'mir' = to me (dative of 'ich')
common_mistakes: Saying 'Ich bin gut' — this implies 'I am morally good', not 'I am well'

usage_notes: Used to greet guests, visitors, or newcomers. 'Herzlich' adds warmth (literally 'heartily').
common_collocations: herzlich willkommen heißen, herzlich willkommen bei uns
image_search_query: open door welcome sign warm family home
image_context: A family at an open door welcoming guests warmly
example_2: Herzlich willkommen, bitte setzen Sie sich.
example_3_translation: Nós o recebemos de boas-vindas.
pronunciation_tips: 'ch' in 'herzlich' is a palatal /ç/ (softer than 'ch' in Bach)
memory_hook: 'Herz' = heart; 'willkommen' resembles 'welcome' — a heartfelt welcome
related_words: das Herz, willkommen, begrüßen, der Gast
common_mistakes: Confusing the /ç/ in 'herzlich' with the /x/ in 'nach' — they are different sounds

secondary_meaning: excuse me? / could you repeat that?
usage_notes: Used when you did not hear or understand something. Polite and neutral tone.
common_collocations: Wie bitte? (questioning tone), Entschuldigung, wie bitte?
image_search_query: person ear cupped listening confused question face
image_context: A person cupping their ear, looking confused and asking for repetition
example_1: Wie bitte? Ich habe das nicht verstanden.
example_2: Wie bitte, können Sie das wiederholen?
example_3_translation: Ele pergunta: 'Como disse?'
pronunciation_tips: Rising intonation turns it into a polite question
memory_hook: 'Wie' = how/what + 'bitte' = please → 'what please?' = 'pardon?'
common_mistakes: Using 'Was?' alone — acceptable but much less polite than 'Wie bitte?'

usage_notes: Casual farewell implying that you will meet again in the near future.
common_collocations: Bis bald!, bis dann, bis morgen
image_search_query: friends waving goodbye see you soon smile street
image_context: Friends waving goodbye on a street, smiling
example_2: Bis bald, ich freue mich darauf.
example_2_translation: Até breve, estou ansioso para isso.
example_3: Wir sagen Bis bald und gehen.
example_3_translation: Dizemos até logo e vamos embora.
pronunciation_tips: Final 'd' in 'bald' is devoiced to /t/
memory_hook: 'Bis' = until; 'bald' = soon → 'until soon'
common_mistakes: Saying 'bis bald' when you won't see the person soon — use 'auf Wiedersehen' instead

usage_notes: Used when parting and expecting to see the person the following day.
common_collocations: Bis morgen früh, Tschüss, bis morgen
image_search_query: person leaving waving goodbye night tomorrow school
image_context: A student waving goodbye outside school in the evening
example_2: Bis morgen früh in der Schule!
example_2_translation: Até amanhã de manhã na escola!
example_3: Sie sagt Bis morgen und geht.
example_3_translation: Ela diz até amanhã e vai embora.
pronunciation_tips: 'Morgen' has a uvular /ʁ/ in standard German
memory_hook: 'morgen' = tomorrow (cognate with English 'morrow'); 'bis' = until
related_words: der Morgen, heute, übermorgen, Bis bald
common_mistakes: Confusing 'morgen' (tomorrow) with 'der Morgen' (the morning) — context clarifies

usage_notes: Used to introduce oneself. Literally 'I am called'. Alternative: 'Ich bin [Name]'.
common_collocations: Ich heiße ... und komme aus, Wie heißen Sie?
image_search_query: person introducing themselves handshake name tag event
image_context: A person introducing themselves with a handshake at an event
example_2: Ich heiße Thomas. Und du?
example_2_translation: Meu nome é Thomas. E você?
example_3: Wie heißen Sie? — Ich heiße Schmidt.
example_3_translation: Qual é o seu nome? — Meu nome é Schmidt.
pronunciation_tips: 'heiße' contains /aɪ̯/ diphthong and ends with /sə/
memory_hook: 'heißen' is related to 'be called/named'; 'heiße' is the first-person form
common_mistakes: Using 'Ich bin heiße' — simply say 'Ich heiße' or 'Ich bin'

usage_notes: Formal question about origin. Informal version: 'Woher kommst du?'
common_collocations: Woher kommen Sie? Ich komme aus ...
image_search_query: world map pointing country origin travel passport
image_context: A person pointing to their home country on a world map
example_1_translation: De onde o senhor é, por favor?
example_2: Woher kommen Sie? — Ich komme aus Brasilien.
example_2_translation: De onde você é? — Sou do Brasil.
example_3_translation: Ela pergunta: 'De onde o senhor vem?'
pronunciation_tips: 'Woher' = /voˈheːʁ/; 'wo' sounds like /vo/, not /wo/
memory_hook: 'wo' = where, 'her' = hither/from — 'where from?'
related_words: kommen, das Land, die Herkunft, wohin
common_mistakes: Answering with 'Ich bin aus' — correct form is 'Ich komme aus'

usage_notes: Essential phrase for language learners. Informal: 'Sprichst du Deutsch?'
common_collocations: Sprechen Sie Englisch?, ein bisschen Deutsch sprechen
image_search_query: German flag language learning books conversation
image_context: Language learning books with a German flag on a desk
example_2: Ja, ich spreche ein bisschen Deutsch.
example_2_translation: Sim, eu falo um pouco de alemão.
example_3_translation: O senhor também fala inglês?
pronunciation_tips: 'Sprechen' starts with /ʃp/ — 'sp' at the start of a word is always /ʃp/ in German
memory_hook: 'sprechen' is related to English 'speak'; 'Deutsch' = German
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'sp' as in English — in German, initial 'sp' = /ʃp/

usage_notes: Critical survival phrase for A1 learners. 'Ich verstehe das nicht' (I don't understand that) adds specificity.
common_collocations: Ich verstehe nicht ganz, leider verstehe ich nicht
image_search_query: confused person question mark language barrier misunderstanding
image_context: A confused person surrounded by question marks, indicating a language barrier
example_2: Ich verstehe das Wort nicht.
example_3: Bitte langsamer — ich verstehe nicht alles.
example_3_translation: Por favor, mais devagar — não entendo tudo.
pronunciation_tips: 'verstehe' stress on second syllable: ver-STE-he
memory_hook: 'verstehen' shares root with English 'understand' in concept; 'stehen' = to stand → to 'stand under' something
common_mistakes: Saying 'ich keine Verstehe' — word order must keep verb second: 'Ich verstehe nicht'

usage_notes: Formal request to repeat. Informal: 'Kannst du das wiederholen?' Essential for classroom and conversation practice.
common_collocations: bitte wiederholen, noch einmal bitte
image_search_query: teacher student repeat classroom pointing board learning
image_context: A student asking a teacher to repeat something at a whiteboard
example_1_translation: O senhor pode repetir isso, por favor?
example_2: Ich habe nicht verstanden. Können Sie das wiederholen?
example_3_translation: Sim, repito com prazer.
pronunciation_tips: 'wieder' = /ˈviːdɐ/; 'holen' = /ˈhoːlən/ — compound verb, stress on 'wie'
memory_hook: 'wieder' = again, 'holen' = to fetch/bring — 'bring it again' = repeat
common_mistakes: Separating the prefix incorrectly — in infinitive form it stays 'wiederholen', not 'holen wieder'

usage_notes: Stronger form of 'Danke'. 'Vielen Dank' = many thanks. Can be followed by 'für + noun' to specify what you are thankful for.
common_collocations: Vielen Dank für alles, Vielen herzlichen Dank
image_search_query: grateful handshake thank you gift warm smile appreciation
image_context: A person receiving a gift and expressing deep gratitude with a handshake
example_2: Vielen Dank, das ist sehr freundlich.
example_3: Er sagt vielen Dank und geht.
example_3_translation: Ele diz muito obrigado e vai embora.
pronunciation_tips: 'vielen' = /ˈfiːlən/; 'v' in German is pronounced /f/
memory_hook: 'viel' = many/much; 'Dank' = thanks — 'many thanks'
common_mistakes: Pronouncing 'v' in 'vielen' as English /v/ — it should be /f/
